evidence_quotes.quote: Would be awesome if DFA could also include protecting programs that were bought (full price, for a product - not subscription or just for an access) but, at time, are being later blocked by producer.

I would like it, that if I paid for something, it is mine to use for how long I want to use it (like we do with cars - if I want to ride a 20-30 yearls old car, it is my choice - as long as it is safe, etc.; let's imagine a car producer that blockes its engine thru the radio / Internet connection, after 5-6 years).

And if such a program becomes unsafe to use (because it - or part of it - works thru the Internet, and after producer stops adding patches - so it works well with operatins system, etc.), then it should be my choice only, if I want to risk getting a virus or getting my PC hacked.
Then, after programs developer decides to stop working on it, it's "connection" server is also closed - leading to whole program being unusable.
Adobe stated about older Photoshop versions: "You are no longer licensed to use the software(...) - in USA they treatened to sue users)"

paragraphs.text: Would be awesome if DFA could also include protecting programs that were bought (full price, for a product - not subscription or just for an access) but, at time, are being later blocked by producer. It happens with all types of programs, like Adobe Photoshop (Adobe stated about older Photoshop versions: "You are no longer licensed to use the software(...) - in USA they treatened to sue users), or some video games. I would like it, that if I paid for something, it is mine to use for how long I want to use it (like we do with cars - if I want to ride a 20-30 yearls old car, it is my choice - as long as it is safe, etc.; let's imagine a car producer that blockes its engine thru the radio / Internet connection, after 5-6 years). Applications / programs producers say, that they are blocking it (one way or another), because it can be unsafe. And if such a program becomes unsafe to use (because it - or part of it - works thru the Internet, and after producer stops adding patches - so it works well with operatins system, etc.), then it should be my choice only, if I want to risk getting a virus or getting my PC hacked. It would be enough, if they just said "we are stopping developing this product - following that, we no longer take any accountability for its safe use". Another examples are programs (also video games), which despite being able to work offline with full capabilities, and made to do exacly that (sinle player games), without a single part that requires Internet connection (example of that would be an on-line chat with other users), require Internet connection (either thru whole time when are being used, or at the start). Then, after programs developer decides to stop working on it, it's "connection" server is also closed - leading to whole program being unusable. Would be great, if by the end of the products 'life', producer made it possible for customers to keep using it - on their own, at their own risk and responsibility. And if the program is an on-line type, after producer drops it (if at all, because if they want to keep developing it - that is great!), let users maintain it on their own - without any profit from it. There is an iniciative whose foundations are such - Stop Killing Games, but it could also work within Digital Fairness Act. Thank You!
